food_subgroup: Crustacean-based prepared dish; tortilla-wrapped prawn enchilada with sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general enchilada class (FOODON_03311737); no prawn/shrimp-specific enchilada subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest parent for prawn-filled tortilla dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for prawn enchiladas. A0FKP (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible parent. The tortilla/sauce component is not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for enchiladas or prawn enchiladas as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of prawn enchiladas consists of two rolled corn tortillas filled with seasoned prawns, topped with enchilada sauce and melted cheese, weighing approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Enchilada with meat and cheese, homemade (SR Legacy ID 21340) adapted for prawn/shrimp filling; supplemented with USDA SR Legacy shrimp cooked data (NDB 15152)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for prawn enchiladas exists. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy enchilada with meat and cheese (per 100 g basis) with protein and fat profile adjusted to reflect prawn filling (USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp, NDB 15152). Cheese and sauce components retained from the enchilada base entry. This is a best-available adaptation from approved sources. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors: protein 4 kcal/g, carbohydrate 4 kcal/g, fat 9 kcal/g.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Corn tortilla GI = 46 (Table entry); mixed enchilada dish estimated at GI 45 based on corn tortilla base and protein/fat dampening effect.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn enchiladas. GI estimated at 45 based on corn tortilla GI of 46 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle. The protein content from prawns (~10 g/100 g) and fat from cheese (~5.8 g/100 g) are known to attenuate postprandial glycemic response, supporting a GI at or slightly below the corn tortilla reference. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 43.5) / 100 = 19.6, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 43.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1260 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit prawn enchiladas to occasional consumption, as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium — over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from enchilada sauce, cheese, and seasoning; requesting reduced-sauce or low-sodium cheese preparations is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~19.6 per serving) from corn tortilla carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as a standard 300 g serving of prawn enchiladas carries a glycemic load of approximately 19.6; the high-protein prawn filling helps moderate postprandial glucose rise, but the corn tortilla and sauce contribute meaningful carbohydrate, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables or reducing to one enchilada can help manage blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and moderate dietary cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, prawn enchiladas present a moderate risk profile: prawns contribute dietary cholesterol (~78 mg/100 g) and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, while the cheese topping adds saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g); choosing reduced-fat cheese and limiting portion to one enchilada can reduce saturated fat intake to within heart-healthy guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn enchiladas are a Mexican-origin dish consisting of corn tortillas rolled around a filling of seasoned cooked prawns (shrimp), then topped with a tomato-chili enchilada sauce and melted cheese before being baked or grilled until heated through. The dish delivers a combination of tender, slightly sweet prawn flesh with the earthy flavour of corn tortilla, the tangy heat of chili-tomato sauce, and the richness of melted cheese. Texturally, the tortilla softens under the sauce while the prawn filling remains firm and succulent. Nutritionally, prawn enchiladas provide a moderate energy density of approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution (~10 g/100 g) from the crustacean filling, moderate carbohydrate (~14.5 g/100 g) from the corn tortilla, and moderate fat (~5.8 g/100 g) largely from cheese. The dish is a notable source of vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus from the prawns, and provides calcium from the cheese. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium content (~420 mg/100 g), which is elevated due to the sauce and cheese, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load per standard serving is moderate (~19.6), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. In Singapore, this dish is served at Mexican-themed restaurants and is classified under the fish and seafood food group due to its prawn-dominant protein source. Common allergens include crustaceans, gluten, and dairy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prawn enchiladas?
What is the protein content of shrimp enchiladas?
Is prawn enchilada high in sodium?
What is the GI of enchiladas?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for prawn enchiladas exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy enchilada with meat and cheese, adjusted for prawn filling using USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data. GI is estimated from the corn tortilla GI reference in Atkinson et al. 2008 (peer-reviewed, University of Sydney GI Database aligned). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy (enchilada with meat/cheese base + cooked shrimp adjustment); no exact prawn enchilada entry exists in any approved source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy enchilada source entry and is not reliably estimated for this dish. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy enchilada source entry for this dish. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are reasonable approximations. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla GI = 46 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for prawn enchiladas exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input food_name 'PRAWN ENCHLADAS' contains a typographical error ('ENCHLADAS'); corrected to 'Prawn Enchiladas' as the canonical English name. 7. Halal status is assumed true as prawns are halal and no pork or alcohol ingredients are standard in this dish; restaurant-level halal certification was not verified. 8. FoodOn match FOODON_03311737 (enchilada) is used as closest parent; no prawn-specific enchilada subclass exists in FoodOn as of April 2026. 9. FoodEx2 code A0FKP (cooked crustaceans) is used as closest parent; no composite enchilada dish code exists in FoodEx2.
